WebSpace ABP rule probe

Drops a sample element for every rule shape we declare support (or non-support) for, waits for the cosmetic shim to settle, then reports per row whether the target's computed display is none. Each section spells out its expected verdict so the green/red coloring is self-explanatory.

OK matches the section's expectation FAIL does NOT match expectation — investigate pending… waiting for shim to settle note host- or network-dependent

Platform: checking… — sub-resource interceptor: checking…
Merged bloom: checking…

1. Supported cosmetic rules

Expected verdict per row: BLOCKED. Every rule in this group is one we declare support for. With an active filter list that contains the rule, the target's computed display should be none. A row reading "visible" means your loaded list either doesn't contain that rule or didn't fire it.
Rule shape Expected Actual Sample markup

1B. uBO :style() rules

Expected verdict per row: declaration applied. The parser converts ##sel:style(decls) into a CSS rule sel { decls } in the same early <style> tag as the hide rules — the matching element should NOT be display:none but its target declaration should be in effect. A row reading "missed" means the parser dropped the rule or your loaded list doesn't carry it.
Rule shape Expected Actual Sample markup

1C. Procedural cosmetic actions

Expected verdict per row: action fired. uBO's procedural operators end in an action other than the default hide: :remove() (drop the element from the DOM), :upward(N):remove() (walk N ancestors first), :style(...) on a procedural selector, :remove-attr(), and :remove-class(). Engine-only — these all read "missed" when the Rust adblock engine is off. The shim runner lives in lib/services/procedural_cosmetic_shim.dart.

Works on both file:// and HTTP origins. The Dart parser captures generic procedural rules (adblock-rust silently drops these at cosmetic_filter_cache_builder.rs:106, so we backfill with our own parsed bucket) and the page-side shim splits embedded ABP pseudos like :has-text() / :upward() out of the css-selector string before feeding the pure-CSS portion to querySelectorAll.
Rule shape Expected Actual Sample markup

2. Host-dependent rules

Expected verdict per row: BLOCKED only when this page is hosted on the rule's domain. Otherwise "visible (host did not match)" is the correct outcome and is colored as a neutral note rather than a failure.
Rule shape Expected Actual Sample markup

3. Skipped rule shapes

Expected verdict per row: visible. These rule shapes are unsupported by design — the parser drops the whole rule line. If a row turns BLOCKED, the parser is now accepting an unsupported rule (e.g. naively splitting at : and keeping the bare class) — that's a regression.
Rule shape (parser must skip) Expected Actual Sample markup

4. False-positive guards

Expected verdict per row: visible. Each row drops markup that looks like a supported rule's target but must not match (substring class, attribute prefix, text extension, missing :has() descendant, etc.). If a row turns BLOCKED, the parser or shim is over-matching — that's a regression.
Selector under test Expected Actual Sample markup (must NOT match)

5. Negative controls

Expected verdict per row: visible. Plain content with no rule that should target it. If any row turns BLOCKED, your filter list is over-matching ordinary markup — file a bug.
Description Expected Actual Sample markup

6. Network rules (heuristic, triangulated)

Expected verdict per row: see "Expected" column. Each rule is probed two ways — <img> via new Image().src and fetch() with mode:'no-cors' — and the performance.getEntriesByName API is consulted to classify the outcome: js-blocked → no Performance entry, request was aborted before networking; net-error → entry exists but server / interceptor returned an error; loaded → request succeeded; net-timeout → entry exists, no response in 5s.

Reading verdicts by platform:
  • iOS / macOS: js-blocked is the block signal — the JS setter wrap declines to call origSet, no network entry is ever created.
  • Android: the native FastSubresourceInterceptor returns a synthetic empty response, so a real block produces a Performance entry and reads as net-error. Compare against the control rows: if controls load and the blocked rows net-error, the rule fired.
Rule Expected img fetch Probe target

7. Page itself, for sanity

If the chrome below is hidden, your filter list is over-matching the test page itself — file a bug.

This paragraph should remain visible. [chrome marker]
